name: Argument Structure: Unaccusative
intuition: The lexical verb encodes a one-participant event in which the sole argument functions as an internal argument (theme/patient), and the verb does not license an external argument.
set_to_1_if: The target verb does not license or introduce an external argument.

The target verb denotes a change-of-state event in which the sole argument functions as an internal argument (theme/patient), and no external argument is licensed (e.g., 'die', 'melt', 'break', 'freeze', 'wither').
The target verb denotes directed motion or change-of-location where the sole argument undergoes displacement and no external argument is licensed (e.g., 'arrive', 'fall', 'leave').
The target verb denotes existence, appearance, or occurrence without licensing an external argument (e.g., 'exist', 'appear', 'happen', 'remain').

Copular clauses with 'be' are marked as unaccusative: the verb does not license an external argument, and the sole overt argument functions as the syntactic subject linked to a predicative complement (e.g., 'has been king', 'has been sick', 'has been in the city').
set_to_0_if: The target verb licenses or introduces an external argument (agent/initiator), whether overtly realized or suppressed.
The clause contains an overt direct or indirect grammatical object introduced by the target verb.
The clause is morphologically passive, and the lexical verb licenses an external argument (whether overtly expressed or suppressed).
examples_positive: He has died. (Intransitive change of state -> 1)
He has arrived. (Change of location without external argument -> 1)
She has been queen for ten years. (Copular 'be' clause -> 1)
examples_negative: She has flown. (Directed motion with external argument only -> 0)
